notes: 40%+ of commercial server buyers consider availability an important buying attribute per Aberdeen field research.
Aberdeen rated HP's end-to-end HA theme as credible based on HP's considerable experience in the space.
Aberdeen estimated a 12-18 month useful window for the end-to-end HA umbrella message.
Aberdeen rated HP's end-to-end HA position as defendable and sustainable.
HP's single-Unix-system availability rated average based on September 1998 DH Brown clustering white paper; conceded by HP's HA marketing team.
Aberdeen recommended HP bundle MC/ServiceGuard as the lowest HA level to improve competitive picture against Sun and IBM.
No NT version of MC/ServiceGuard existed; gap confirmed as unfixable for technology reasons.
Aberdeen recommended HP acquire Marathon Technologies for FT-on-NT capability to leverage systems and service revenues.
HP did not acquire Marathon. Marathon was eventually acquired by Stratus Technologies circa 2005.
Aberdeen recommended transitioning from 'five nines' jargon toward broader 'end-to-end high availability' super-message.
Lowest understanding of 'five nines' was among line-of-business executives and CEO/CFO/COO management.
IBM understood HA technologies but had not unified HA messaging across four hardware platforms nor pursued end-to-end beyond custom Global Services projects.
Sun rated poor-to-fair in clustering with single-box HA focus, lacking global services comparable to IBM and HP.
Sun lacked any concept of end-to-end HA.
Compaq limited HA thrust to rack-mounted clusters. Digital services not widely deployed in HA.
Compaq had not worked on end-to-end HA.
Tandem division of Compaq focused on selling to installed base with no likely change in focus, despite deep HA expertise.
Aberdeen recommended broadening HA scope beyond Unix OLTP to include email, data warehouses, e-business, supply chain, and call centers.
NT and Unix products and services should approach parity longer term. In 1999-2000 HP must clearly communicate Intel/NT HA limits.
Windows Server 2003 and later editions significantly improved HA capabilities, confirming the prediction of gradual improvement.
HAO remote monitoring identified as one of three competitive differentiators for HP's HA positioning.
HP's HA advantage had a high level of services components, raising the bar for box-technology-only competitors like Sun and Compaq.
HP's HA message must cover both directly sold and indirectly sold channels.
Vertical focus of HA starts with telcos, finance, and retail in-store systems.
End-to-end HA requires ISV applications to plug into HP HA infrastructure; this represents an HP-only investment that ISVs find generally unpalatable.
End-to-end HA concept requires deeper relationships with partners like Cisco.
End-to-end HA concept requires deeper relationships with partners like EMC.
Improvements in 1999 expected to make HP's offering on single systems more competitive.
Sun Microsystems acquired by Oracle in 2010. Its single-box HA focus and lack of services vision contributed to declining competitiveness.
Compaq acquired by HP in 2002. Tandem NonStop technology absorbed into HP and continues as HPE NonStop.
MC/ServiceGuard evolved into HPE Serviceguard, still available on HPE servers for HP-UX and Linux.
End-to-end availability thinking became mainstream through cloud-native resilience engineering, SRE practices, and distributed systems design.
Five nines remains a standard metric but SLA-driven language and error budgets (as in SRE) became the broader communication framework, partially validating Aberdeen's recommendation.
Digital Equipment Corporation acquired by Compaq in 1998. Referenced via Compaq's Digital services capability.
Aberdeen field research covered both NT and Unix markets for server buyer availability importance.
